extra.reasoning:
The property requires that (rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b) is never jointly true.

rec_drug_a: Only r1 (priority 50) can set it true, IF (hyperkalemia AND bradycardia). Otherwise default false. So rec_drug_a is true exactly when hyperkalemia AND bradycardia.

rec_drug_b: r2 (priority 50) sets it true IF hyperkalemia; r5 (priority 90) sets it false IF (hyperkalemia AND bradycardia). Per-output highest-priority matching rule wins. When both hyperkalemia AND bradycardia hold, both r2 and r5 match, but r5 (priority 90) beats r2 (priority 50), so rec_drug_b := false.

Therefore in the only scenario where rec_drug_a is true (hyperkalemia AND bradycardia), r5 fires and forces rec_drug_b to false. They can never both be true.

Enumerating the (hyperkalemia, bradycardia) cases (renal_impairment and hypertension only affect rec_drug_c, irrelevant here):
- (T,T): rec_drug_a=true, rec_drug_b=false (r5 overrides r2) -> no conflict
- (T,F): rec_drug_a=false (default), rec_drug_b=true (r2) -> no conflict
- (F,T): rec_drug_a=false, rec_drug_b=false -> no conflict
- (F,F): rec_drug_a=false, rec_drug_b=false -> no conflict

No input makes both true. The high-priority r5 override is exactly the safety mechanism that suppresses drug b whenever drug a is recommended. The property holds for the entire input space.
